preamble: The glottal stop, a sound produced by briefly obstructing airflow at the vocal cords, can undergo a transformation into the voiced labio-velar approximant /w/. This shift often involves a 'weakening' or 'softening' of the consonant, where the complete closure of the glottal stop gives way to a more open, vowel-like articulation at the lips and velum.
phoneticEffects: Lenition (plosive to approximant), debuccalization (loss of glottal articulation), and labialization/velarization (acquisition of labio-velar articulation)

examples.note: In the Arabic variety of Nefza (Northwest Tunisia), some verbs historically beginning with a glottal stop have developed an initial /w/ in both perfect and imperfect forms, classifying them as primae w verbs.
In certain Old Arabic varieties, such as the dialects of Hudhail (east of Mecca) and Taghlib (Iraq), there is documented evidence of the weakening of an initial hamza (glottal stop) to /w/ or /y/.

sources: Aguado, J. (2021). The Arabic Variety of Nefza (Northwest Tunisia): Phonology, Morphology and Texts. Universidad de Zaragoza.
Owens, J. (2013). A Linguistic History of Arabic. Oxford University Press.
